title: Vibes are winning, whether you like it or not
gist: A short (paywalled) teaser argues that smartphones have democratized skill acquisition once gated by teachers and cookbooks, and frames current politics as a fight over a new kind of 'platform' where no institution is left to preserve the truth.

insights.claim: Skills that once required a cookbook, a class, or a family member to teach — such as filleting a fish, proper squat form, sourdough baking, or watercolor painting — can now be learned directly from a phone, reflecting a shift in how expertise has been transmitted over roughly the last 30 years.
insights.speaker: Halina Bennet
insights.so_what: Signals a broader collapse of traditional gatekeepers of knowledge and instruction, a dynamic the piece implies extends into how political 'truth' and authority are now transmitted and contested.
insights.quote: Less than 30 years ago, if you wanted to learn how to fillet a fish, you needed a cookbook, a class, or a family member or friend to teach you. Today, you just need a phone.
insights.locator: Less than 30 years ago, if you wanted to learn how to fillet a fish

insights.claim: The article's framing thesis is that politics today is fundamentally a contest over a different kind of 'platform,' and that no institution or actor is left to safeguard factual truth in that contest.
insights.speaker: Halina Bennet
insights.so_what: Suggests investors tracking media, platforms, and political risk should weight informal/attention-based platforms over traditional institutional credibility as arbiters of political narrative.
insights.quote: Politics is about a different kind of platform, and no one is around to preserve the truth.

insights.claim: A photo accompanying the piece shows Joe Rogan attending an executive order signing in the Oval Office, illustrating how a prominent independent-media/podcast figure has become physically embedded in formal presidential proceedings.
insights.speaker: Halina Bennet
insights.so_what: A visible marker of how new-media personalities have gained direct proximity to executive power, relevant to anyone tracking the political influence of podcast/independent-media figures.
